keyPoints: Initiate routine diabetes screening at age 35, repeat every 3 years
Screen individuals with HIV medications or glucocorticoids more frequently
Cystic fibrosis: begin screening at age 10 using OGTT
Post-transplantation patients should use OGTT for screening
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · SCREENING | TABLE 415-2 Criteria for Screening for Type 2 DM in Adults

items.subtopic: OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS
items.title: Insulin's Role in Glucose Homeostasis
items.content: Insulin is the primary regulator of glucose homeostasis, balancing energy intake and utilization. In fasting states, low insulin levels promote hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis while reducing peripheral tissue uptake. Postprandially, insulin facilitates glucose disposal primarily by skeletal muscle through stimulated uptake.
keyPoints: Insulin regulates metabolic equilibrium with neural input and other hormones like glucagon.
In fasting: low insulin + modest glucagon increase hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis.
Postprandially in healthy individuals, insulin rises while glucagon falls to optimize glucose disposal.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS

items.subtopic: OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS
items.title: Insulin Biosynthesis and Secretion
items.content: Insulin is synthesized as preproinsulin in pancreatic beta cells, processed to proinsulin, then cleaved into insulin and C-peptide. C-peptide serves as a marker of endogenous insulin secretion due to its slower clearance compared to insulin.
keyPoints: Insulin synthesis begins with an 86-amino-acid precursor, preproinsulin.
Proteolytic processing yields proinsulin, which is cleaved into A and B chains connected by disulfide bonds.
C-peptide, cleared more slowly than insulin, indicates endogenous insulin secretion.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS

items.title: Mechanism of Insulin Secretion and Its Regulation
items.content: Insulin secretion is primarily regulated by glucose levels, with a threshold >3.9 mmol/L (70 mg/dL) initiating the process. This involves glucose transport into beta cells via facilitative glucose transporters, followed by phosphorylation by glucokinase. The rate-limiting step in this pathway is crucial for controlling insulin release. Glucose metabolism leads to ATP production, which inhibits ATP-sensitive K+ channels, causing membrane depolarization and subsequent calcium influx that stimulates insulin secretion.
keyPoints: Glucose levels >3.9 mmol/L (70 mg/dL) stimulate insulin synthesis.
Glucokinase phosphorylation is the rate-limiting step in glucose-regulated insulin secretion.
ATP inhibits ATP-sensitive K+ channels, leading to membrane depolarization and calcium influx.
Voltage-dependent calcium channels open upon depolarization, stimulating insulin release.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN SECRETION

items.title: Role of Incretin Hormones in Insulin Secretion
items.content: Incretin hormones such as GLP-1 and GIP enhance insulin secretion when blood glucose levels are above fasting. They bind to specific receptors on beta cells, increasing cyclic AMP production and stimulating insulin release. Additionally, these hormones suppress glucagon secretion. Incretin analogues or agents that prolong endogenous GLP-1 activity are used in treating type 2 diabetes.
keyPoints: GLP-1 and GIP stimulate insulin secretion only when blood glucose is above fasting levels.
Incretins increase cyclic AMP production to enhance insulin release.
They also suppress glucagon production.
Incretin analogues are used in the treatment of type 2 diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN SECRETION

items.title: Insulin's Role in Glucose Metabolism
items.content: Insulin, secreted into the portal venous system, suppresses hepatic glucose production and enhances hepatic glucose uptake. It is cleared by the liver with a 50% first-pass effect, creating a portal-to-peripheral gradient of ~2:1. In systemic circulation, insulin binds to receptors in peripheral tissues, initiating signaling cascades crucial for glucose uptake, glycogen synthesis, protein synthesis, lipogenesis, and gene regulation.
keyPoints: Insulin is secreted into the portal venous system.
50% of insulin is cleared by the liver on first pass.
Portal-to-peripheral insulin concentration gradient is ~2:1.
Insulin receptor activation leads to metabolic effects via pathways like PI3-kinase, crucial for GLUT4 translocation and glucose uptake.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN ACTION | TYPE1 DM

items.title: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus Pathogenesis
items.content: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (DM) results from genetic, environmental, and immunologic factors leading to immune-mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells. It often presents acutely with symptoms like diabetic ketoacidosis in 25-50% of cases. Onset can occur at any age, with up to 40% developing after age 30, sometimes misdiagnosed as type 2 DM.
keyPoints: Type 1 DM results from immune-mediated beta cell destruction.
25–50% present with diabetic ketoacidosis.
Onset can occur at any age; up to 40% onset after age 30.
Misdiagnosis with type 2 DM is common in adults.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN ACTION | TYPE1 DM

items.title: Autoimmunity and Beta Cell Decline in Type 1 DM
items.content: Most individuals with type 1 DM show evidence of islet-directed autoimmunity, detected by autoantibodies against islet cell antigens. The presence of two or more autoantibodies indicates stage 1 type 1 DM. There is a gradual decline in beta cell function and mass before clinical diagnosis, often accelerated ~1-2 years prior to Stage 3 onset.
keyPoints: Islet-directed autoimmunity detected by autoantibodies.
Two or more autoantibodies indicate stage 1 type 1 DM.
Initial loss of first-phase insulin response precedes Stage 3 onset.
~1-2 years before Stage 3, there is accelerated beta cell decline.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN ACTION | TYPE1 DM

items.title: Genetic Susceptibility and Concordance in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Type 1 DM susceptibility involves multiple genes, with the HLA region on chromosome 6 being a major contributor. The concordance rate for type 1 DM in identical twins is between 30-70%, indicating other modifying factors are involved.
keyPoints: Concordance of type 1 DM in identical twins ranges from 30–70%.
HLA region on chromosome 6 accounts for ~50% of genetic risk.
Predisposing HLA haplotypes include DRB1*0301-DQB1*0201 (DR3-DQ2) and DRB1*0401-DQB1*0302 (DR4-DQ8).
Risk in relatives: 1–9% if the parent has type 1 DM, 6–7% for a sibling.
>80% of individuals with type 1 DM do not have a relative with the disorder.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic and Environmental Interplay in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Beyond MHC class II associations, over 60 additional genetic loci contribute to type 1 DM susceptibility. Recent trends show a shift with decreased representation of highest-risk HLA alleles and increased penetrance in lower-risk genotypes, suggesting environmental factors' growing influence.
keyPoints: >60 additional genetic loci identified (e.g., insulin gene promoter, CTLA-4 gene, interleukin 2 receptor, PTPN22).
Polygenic risk scores are used to predict type 1 DM risk in population-based studies.
Decreased representation of highest-risk HLA alleles and increased penetrance in lower-risk genotypes observed in recent cohorts.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Islet Cell Autoantibodies (ICAs) in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Islet cell autoantibodies are crucial markers for identifying type 1 diabetes mellitus. They are present in over 85% of individuals with new-onset type 1 DM and can predict the risk of developing the disease in high-risk children.
keyPoints: ICAs are directed at pancreatic islet molecules such as insulin, GAD, IA-2, or ZnT8.
>85% presence of ICAs in individuals with new-onset type 1 DM.
Presence of two or more ICAs in high-risk children indicates a nearly 70% risk of developing type 1 DM after 10 years and >80% risk after 15 years.
ICAs are used to define the onset of stage 1 type 1 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Beta Cell Destruction in Type 1 Diabetes
items.content: Beta cell destruction in type 1 diabetes is primarily mediated by CD8+ T cell-mediated cytotoxicity and exacerbated by cytokine effects and reactive oxygen species.
keyPoints: Direct CD8+ T cell-mediated cytotoxicity is the main mechanism of beta cell death.
Cytokines like TNF-α, interferon γ, and IL-1 contribute to beta cell damage.
Reactive oxygen species generated by immune cells also play a role in beta cell destruction.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Intervention with Teplizumab for High-Risk Individuals
items.content: Teplizumab, an anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody, can delay the onset of stage 3 type 1 DM in high-risk individuals by a median of 32.5 months.
keyPoints: Teplizumab is used for high-risk individuals with multiple autoantibodies and dysglycemia (stage 2 type 1 DM).
FDA-approved for stage 2 type 1 DM to delay progression to clinical diabetes.
Median delay of onset of stage 3 type 1 DM by 32.5 months.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Insulin Resistance and Secretion in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is characterized by hyperglycemia due to insulin resistance and abnormal insulin secretion. Insulin resistance typically precedes the defect in insulin secretion, leading to T2DM when insulin production becomes insufficient relative to the level of insulin resistance.
keyPoints: Insulin resistance often precedes insulin secretory defects.
Diabetes develops when insulin secretion is inadequate for the degree of insulin resistance.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · TYPE2DM

items.title: Ethnic Variations in Type 2 Diabetes Pathophysiology
items.content: There are ethnic differences in the pathophysiology of T2DM. Latinos generally exhibit greater insulin resistance, while East Asians and South Asians show more beta cell dysfunction. Both defects are present across these populations, but East and South Asians tend to develop T2DM at a younger age and lower BMI.
keyPoints: Latinos have greater insulin resistance.
East Asians and South Asians exhibit more beta cell dysfunction.
East and South Asians develop T2DM at a younger age and lower BMI.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · TYPE2DM

items.title: Genetic Risk and Concordance in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Type 2 DM has a significant genetic component, with concordance rates of 70-90% among identical twins. The risk for individuals with one diabetic parent is increased, reaching up to 70% if both parents have type 2 DM.
keyPoints: Concordance in identical twins: 70-90%
Risk with one diabetic parent: Increased
Risk with two diabetic parents: Approaches 70%
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Loci and Predictive Challenges in Type 2 DM
items.content: Over 600 genetic loci have been identified that confer a small risk for type 2 DM, with the most prominent being a variant of the transcription factor 7-like 2 gene. However, these loci account for less than 10% of the genetic risk and cannot reliably predict type 2 DM.
keyPoints: Genetic loci identified: >600
Relative risk per locus: 1.06–1.5
<10% of genetic risk determined by known loci
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology and Metabolic Abnormalities in Type 2 DM
items.content: Type 2 DM is characterized by impaired insulin secretion, insulin resistance, and excessive hepatic glucose production. Insulin resistance results from genetic susceptibility, obesity, and metabolic inflammation, leading to reduced glucose utilization.
keyPoints: Insulin resistance: Glucose utilization 30-60% lower than nondiabetic
Obesity prevalence in type 2 DM patients: ≥80%
Pathophysiology includes: Impaired insulin secretion, insulin resistance, excessive hepatic glucose production
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Adipokines in Insulin Resistance
items.content: Adipocytes secrete various biologic products that influence insulin sensitivity. Increased production of free fatty acids and certain adipokines, such as TNF-α, resistin, IL-6, can cause insulin resistance in skeletal muscle and liver.
keyPoints: Adipocytes secrete nonesterified free fatty acids, retinol-binding protein 4, leptin, TNF-$ \alpha $, resistin, IL-6, adiponectin.
Increased production of free fatty acids and some adipokines may cause insulin resistance in skeletal muscle and liver.
Adiponectin levels are reduced in obesity, contributing to hepatic insulin resistance.
Markers of inflammation like IL-6 and C-reactive protein are often elevated in type 2 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Impaired Insulin Secretion in Type 2 Diabetes
items.content: In type 2 diabetes, insulin secretion initially increases to compensate for insulin resistance but eventually declines. This decline is due to a combination of genetic defects and metabolic stressors like glucose toxicity and lipotoxicity.
keyPoints: Insulin secretion increases initially in response to insulin resistance.
The first secretory phase of glucose-stimulated insulin secretion is reduced by up to 50% at onset.
Proinsulin processing abnormalities are reflected by increased proinsulin secretion.
Islet amyloid deposits may worsen beta cell function, though their role as primary or secondary events is unclear.
Chronic hyperglycemia impairs islet function (glucose toxicity).
Elevated free fatty acids and proinflammatory cytokines can worsen islet function.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Increased Hepatic Glucose and Lipid Production in Type 2 Diabetes
items.content: Insulin resistance in the liver leads to increased gluconeogenesis, contributing to fasting hyperglycemia. This is accompanied by dyslipidemia due to enhanced VLDL-triglyceride synthesis.
keyPoints: Insulin resistance in the liver results in failure to suppress gluconeogenesis.
Increased hepatic glucose production occurs early in diabetes.
Dyslipidemia includes elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL, and increased small dense LDL particles.
Retained lipids can lead to metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD).
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Severe Insulin Resistance Syndromes
items.content: Severe insulin resistance can manifest in two distinct syndromes: type A and type B. Type A primarily affects young women, presenting with severe hyperinsulinemia, obesity, and hyperandrogenism due to an undefined defect in the insulin-signaling pathway. Type B affects middle-aged women, characterized by similar features but also includes autoimmune disorders, where autoantibodies against the insulin receptor can block or stimulate it, causing intermittent hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: Type A syndrome: severe hyperinsulinemia, obesity, hyperandrogenism, undefined defect in insulin-signaling pathway.
Type B syndrome: severe hyperinsulinemia, hyperandrogenism, autoimmune disorders, autoantibodies against insulin receptor causing intermittent hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3)

items.title: Prevention Strategies for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is often preceded by impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) or impaired fasting glucose (IFG). Lifestyle modifications and pharmacologic agents can prevent or delay its onset. The Diabetes Prevention Program showed that lifestyle changes, including diet and exercise, reduced the development of type 2 DM by 58% compared to placebo. Metformin also delayed diabetes by 31%. These interventions are effective across various demographics and have long-lasting effects.
keyPoints: Type 2 DM preceded by IGT or IFG.
Lifestyle changes (diet, exercise) prevent/treat type 2 DM by 58%.
Metformin prevents/treats type 2 DM by 31%.
Interventions effective regardless of age, sex, ethnic group.
Effects persist for at least 15 years.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3)

items.title: Monogenic Diabetes and Its Management
items.content: Monogenic diabetes, including MODY types caused by mutations in genes like HNF-1α, HNF-1β, and glucokinase, presents with distinct clinical features and management strategies. For instance, individuals with HNF-1α mutations (MODY 3) may respond well to sulfonylureas despite a progressive decline in glycemic control.
keyPoints: Monogenic diabetes is caused by gene mutations affecting transcription factors or enzymes like glucokinase.
HNF-1α mutation (MODY 3) patients often respond to sulfonylureas, unlike those with HNF-1β mutations who require insulin.
Glucokinase mutations lead to stable hyperglycemia that does not typically need treatment except during pregnancy.
Genetic screening is recommended for individuals with atypical features of type 1 or type 2 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 1)

items.title: Permanent Neonatal Diabetes and Genetic Mutations
items.content: Permanent neonatal diabetes, diagnosed before 6 months of age, is often caused by genetic mutations impacting beta cell function. Activating mutations in ATP-sensitive potassium channel subunits can be treated with sulfonylureas, potentially allowing discontinuation of insulin.
keyPoints: Neonatal diabetes onset occurs <6 months and may overlap phenotypically with type 1 DM.
Activating mutations in Kir6.2 or ABCC8 allow treatment with sulfonylureas instead of insulin.
GATA6 mutations are a common cause of pancreatic agenesis, while homozygous glucokinase mutations lead to severe neonatal diabetes.
Genetic screening is crucial for individuals diagnosed with diabetes before 6 months.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 1)

items.title: COVID-19 and Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: During the COVID-19 pandemic, hyperglycemia was more prevalent among those infected, and existing diabetes worsened outcomes. While SARS-CoV-2 does not directly cause type 1 or type 2 DM, it may increase disease ascertainment and exacerbate hyperglycemia in undiagnosed individuals.
keyPoints: Hyperglycemia is more common in COVID-19 patients, worsening clinical outcomes for those with diabetes.
SARS-CoV-2 does not directly cause type 1 or type 2 DM but may increase ascertainment and worsen hyperglycemia.
Early pandemic data suggested an increased incidence of type 1 DM, though this is likely due to heightened disease recognition.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus
items.title: Pancreatic Exocrine Disease and its Association with Diabetes
items.content: Pancreatic exocrine diseases, including pancreatitis and pancreatic cancer, increase the risk of diabetes, termed type 3c or pancreaticogenic DM. This form involves damage to pancreatic islets leading to insulin and glucagon deficiency.
keyPoints: Type 3c DM results from pancreatic islet damage or destruction.
Pancreatic exocrine diseases like pancreatitis should prompt diabetes screening.
Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes prevalence has risen to almost 50% due to improved survival rates.
Insulin therapy is preferred for most forms of pancreatic diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus
items.title: Immune Checkpoint-Related Diabetes
items.content: A rare form of diabetes occurs after immune checkpoint therapy, such as anti-PD-1 or anti-CTLA-4. This insulin-deficient diabetes can rapidly onset with DKA and shares some features with type 1 DM.
keyPoints: Occurs in <1% of individuals treated with immune checkpoint therapies like anti-PD-1 or anti-CTLA-4.
Rapid onset of hyperglycemia, often with DKA.
Shares genetic similarities and occasional presence of ICA with type 1 DM.
Insulin therapy is required for management.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus

items.title: Diabetes Mellitus: Key Historical and Physical Examination Aspects for Diagnosis and Management
items.content: A thorough medical history focusing on diabetes-relevant aspects is crucial in diagnosing and managing diabetes mellitus (DM). This includes assessing weight changes, family history of DM, cardiovascular risk factors, and symptoms indicative of hyperglycemia. For established DM patients, reviewing the initial diagnosis details, prior therapies, HbA1c levels, glucose monitoring results, frequency of hypoglycemia, and complications is essential for proper management. Physical examination should emphasize weight/BMI assessment, retinal and foot examinations, and checking insulin injection sites.
keyPoints: Symptoms of hyperglycemia include polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, weakness, blurry vision, frequent superficial infections, and slow healing of skin lesions.
Metabolic derangements relate to osmotic diuresis and catabolic state with urinary glucose/calorie loss and muscle breakdown.
Blurred vision due to hyperglycemia resolves as it is controlled.
Review initial DM diagnosis symptoms to confirm correct type of DM.
Assess prior diabetes care, including therapies tried, HbA1c levels, self-monitoring glucose results, hypoglycemia frequency (<3.0 mmol/L, <54 mg/dL), and complications.
Check for DM-related comorbidities like cardiovascular disease, hypertension, dyslipidemia.
Counsel women of childbearing age on tight glycemic control (HbA1c <6.5%) prior to conception.
Physical exam should include weight/BMI, retinal examination, orthostatic blood pressure, foot examination, peripheral pulses, and insulin injection sites.

Annual foot examination includes assessing blood flow, sensation (vibratory with 128-Hz tuning fork, touch with a 5.07, 10-g monofilament), pinprick sensation, ankle reflexes, nail care, and identifying potential ulceration sites.
Screen for distal symmetric polyneuropathy annually from initial diagnosis; screen for autonomic neuropathy 5 years post type 1 DM diagnosis and at type 2 DM diagnosis.

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
items.title: Consider Type 1 Diabetes in Younger Patients with Specific Features
items.content: In patients under 35 years old presenting with new-onset diabetes, consider type 1 diabetes if they are nonobese and exhibit ketoacidosis or have a family history of autoimmune diseases. These features suggest the need for further investigation into immune markers.
keyPoints: Younger age at diagnosis (<35 years) suggests type 1 DM
Nonobese BMI is indicative of type 1 DM in younger patients
Ketoacidosis and markedly elevated plasma glucose are red flags for type 1 DM
Family or personal history of autoimmune disorders prompts consideration of type 1 DM
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
items.title: Identify Type 2 Diabetes in Obese Adults with Associated Conditions
items.content: In adults, obesity (80% prevalence) and associated conditions like insulin resistance, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, dyslipidemia, or PCOS suggest type 2 diabetes. Initial treatment may not require insulin.
keyPoints: Obesity is a common feature in type 2 DM
Associated conditions include insulin resistance, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, dyslipidemia, and PCOS
Type 2 DM patients often do not need initial insulin therapy
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
items.title: Recognize Atypical Diabetes Forms for Precision Medicine
items.content: Some individuals with diabetes do not fit neatly into type 1 or type 2 categories. Consider monogenic diabetes in those diagnosed early, and recognize atypical forms like ketosis-prone type 2 DM or autoimmune diabetes of adults.
keyPoints: Monogenic diabetes should be considered in early-onset cases
Ketosis-prone type 2 DM presents with DKA but lacks autoimmune markers
Autoimmune diabetes of adults may initially appear as type 2 but have GAD and other ICAs
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT

items.title: Diagnosing Diabetes Mellitus: Importance of Glycemic Control and Associated Conditions
items.content: Laboratory assessment for diabetes mellitus (DM) begins with confirming the diagnosis using established criteria, followed by evaluating glycemic control. It is crucial to screen for conditions associated with DM such as albuminuria, dyslipidemia, and thyroid dysfunction.
keyPoints: Assess diagnostic criteria for DM
Evaluate degree of glycemic control
Screen for DM-associated conditions: albuminuria, dyslipidemia, thyroid dysfunction
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.title: Differentiating Types of Diabetes Mellitus Using Laboratory Tests
items.content: Serum C-peptide levels can aid in differentiating between types of DM when interpreted alongside blood glucose levels. Low C-peptide with elevated blood glucose suggests a need for insulin, but it cannot definitively distinguish type 1 from type 2 DM due to residual C-peptide production in some type 1 cases. Islet cell antibodies may be measured at diabetes onset if the type remains unclear.
keyPoints: Serum C-peptide useful with concurrent blood glucose level
Low C-peptide and elevated blood glucose indicate insulin need
C-peptide levels cannot fully distinguish type 1 from type 2 DM
Islet cell antibodies can help when DM type is unclear
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.statement: Approximately 22.8% of adults with diabetes in the United States were undiagnosed as of 2021.
items.explanation: In the United States, approximately 22.8% of adults with diabetes were undiagnosed in 2021.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · ATYPICAL DIABETES | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: The World Health Organization defines impaired fasting glucose (IFG) as an FPG >6.1 mmol/L.
items.explanation: The WHO defines IFG as an FPG >6.1 mmol/L (110 mg/dL).
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: An oral glucose tolerance test is commonly used in routine clinical care for diagnosing diabetes.
items.explanation: The OGTT is not often used in routine clinical care except for pregnancy and screening for gestational or cystic fibrosis-related diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: SCREENING | TABLE 415-2 Criteria for Screening for Type 2 DM in Adults
items.statement: Individuals with impaired fasting glucose (IFG) or impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) should be screened annually.
items.explanation: Annual screening is recommended for individuals with IFG, IGT, or a hemoglobin A1c of 5.7–6.4%.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · SCREENING | TABLE 415-2 Criteria for Screening for Type 2 DM in Adults

items.subtopic: SCREENING | TABLE 415-2 Criteria for Screening for Type 2 DM in Adults
items.statement: Screening for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is discussed in this chapter.
items.explanation: The passage states that screening for GDM is not discussed in this chapter.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · SCREENING | TABLE 415-2 Criteria for Screening for Type 2 DM in Adults

items.subtopic: OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS
items.statement: In the fasting state, insulin levels are high to promote glucose uptake in peripheral tissues.
items.explanation: In the fasting state, insulin levels are low, which reduces glucose uptake in insulin-sensitive tissues.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS

items.subtopic: OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS
items.statement: C-peptide is cleared more slowly than insulin and serves as a marker of endogenous insulin secretion.
items.explanation: C-peptide's slower clearance compared to insulin makes it useful for distinguishing between endogenous and exogenous sources of insulin.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS

items.statement: Impaired first-phase insulin responses are among the earliest detectable abnormalities in type 1 diabetes.
items.explanation: Impaired first-phase insulin responses are among the earliest detectable abnormalities during the progression of both type 1 and type 2 diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN SECRETION

items.statement: GLP-1 release occurs solely from neuroendocrine L-cells of the gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: Recent studies suggest that intraislet production of GLP-1 from alpha cells may also play a role in insulin secretion regulation.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN SECRETION

items.statement: Type 1 DM always presents with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) at diagnosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that 25–50% of individuals present with DKA, not all.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN ACTION | TYPE1 DM

items.statement: Type 1 DM can develop at any age.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that type 1 DM can develop at any age, including over the age of 30.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN ACTION | TYPE1 DM

items.statement: The concordance rate for type 1 DM in identical twins is over 90%.
items.explanation: The concordance of type 1 DM in identical twins ranges from 30–70%, not over 90%.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Most individuals with predisposing HLA haplotypes for type 1 DM develop the disease.
items.explanation: Most individuals with predisposing HLA haplotypes do not develop diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Islet cell autoantibodies directly cause beta cell death in type 1 diabetes mellitus.
items.explanation: ICAs serve as a marker but do not have a direct role in beta cell death.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Environmental factors like viruses and bovine milk proteins have been conclusively linked to the onset of type 1 diabetes mellitus.
items.explanation: No environmental factor has been conclusively linked to triggering type 1 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is characterized by hyperglycemia due to insulin resistance and abnormal insulin secretion.
items.explanation: The common phenotype of Type 2 DM includes hyperglycemia, which results from insulin resistance and abnormal insulin secretion.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · TYPE2DM

items.statement: Type 2 diabetes mellitus develops only when there is an adequate level of insulin secretion for the degree of insulin resistance.
items.explanation: Diabetes develops when insulin secretion becomes inadequate relative to the degree of insulin resistance.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · TYPE2DM

items.statement: Genome-wide association studies have identified over 600 genetic loci that confer a high risk for type 2 DM.
items.explanation: The identified loci convey a relatively small risk for type 2 DM, with a relative risk of 1.06–1.5.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes mellitus is primarily due to reduced insulin receptor levels and tyrosine kinase activity.
items.explanation: These alterations are most likely secondary to hyperinsulinemia, not a primary defect.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: In type 2 DM, insulin secretion initially decreases to maintain normal glucose tolerance.
items.explanation: In type 2 DM, insulin secretion initially increases in response to insulin resistance to maintain normal glucose tolerance.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Islet amyloid polypeptide deposits are definitively known as a primary event in the progression of type 2 DM.
items.explanation: Whether islet amyloid deposits are a primary or secondary event in type 2 DM is not known.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Lipodystrophies are associated with a gain of adipose tissue.
items.explanation: Lipodystrophies involve selective loss of adipose tissue, not gain.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: The Diabetes Prevention Program demonstrated that lifestyle changes can prevent or delay the onset of type 2 DM.
items.explanation: The DPP showed a 58% reduction in developing type 2 DM with lifestyle modifications.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: Mutations in MODY-associated genes are a common cause of type 2 diabetes.
items.explanation: Studies suggest that mutations in MODY-associated genes are a relatively uncommon cause of type 2 DM (<5%).
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 1)

items.statement: Homozygous glucokinase mutations can lead to severe neonatal diabetes.
items.explanation: Homozygous glucokinase mutations cause a severe form of neonatal diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus
items.statement: Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes prevalence is almost 50% due to improved survival.
items.explanation: Improved survival with cystic fibrosis has led to a rise in its related diabetes prevalence.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus
items.statement: Type 1 DM and immune checkpoint-related DM have identical pathogenesis.
items.explanation: Immune checkpoint-related DM likely has a different, undetermined pathogenesis from type 1 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus

items.statement: Blurred vision in diabetes mellitus resolves as hyperglycemia is controlled.
items.explanation: Blurred vision results from changes in the water content of the lens and resolves with controlled hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: Annual screening for autonomic neuropathy is recommended at diagnosis for type 1 DM.
items.explanation: Autonomic neuropathy screening begins 5 years after the initial diagnosis of type 1 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
items.statement: Most individuals with new-onset type 1 DM are lean and nonobese.
items.explanation: Many individuals with new-onset type 1 DM are now overweight or obese.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
items.statement: The age of diagnosis for type 2 DM is increasing.
items.explanation: Although most diagnosed with type 2 DM are older, the age of diagnosis is declining.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT

items.statement: Measurement of islet cell antibodies can help clarify the type of diabetes mellitus when it's not clear from other characteristics.
items.explanation: Islet cell antibody measurement may be useful at diabetes onset if the DM type is unclear.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.statement: Serum C-peptide levels can completely distinguish between type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus.
items.explanation: C-peptide cannot fully differentiate between type 1 and type 2 DM as some with type 1 retain production.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT

items.subtopic: Diabetes Mellitus: Diagnosis, Classification, and Pathophysiology | CLASSIFICATION
items.assertion: Persons with diabetes are at increased risk for cardiovascular disease.
items.reason: Cardiovascular disease is the main cause of morbidity and mortality in individuals with diabetes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why persons with diabetes have an increased risk.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · Diabetes Mellitus: Diagnosis, Classification, and Pathophysiology | CLASSIFICATION

items.subtopic: ATYPICAL DIABETES | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.assertion: The prevalence of type 2 DM is highest in certain Pacific islands and the Middle East.
items.reason: This variability is likely due to genetic, behavioral, and environmental factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · ATYPICAL DIABETES | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: ATYPICAL DIABETES | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.assertion: Diabetes is a major cause of mortality worldwide.
items.reason: In 2021, diabetes was listed as the eighth leading cause of death in the United States.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not fully explain the global impact of diabetes on mortality.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · ATYPICAL DIABETES | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SCREENING | TABLE 415-2 Criteria for Screening for Type 2 DM in Adults
items.assertion: Screening for type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM) should begin at age 35 years.
items.reason: Type 2 DM may be present for up to a decade before diagnosis, and early screening can help identify asymptomatic individuals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why screening should begin at age 35.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · SCREENING | TABLE 415-2 Criteria for Screening for Type 2 DM in Adults

items.subtopic: OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS
items.assertion: Insulin is co-secreted with C-peptide from pancreatic beta cells.
items.reason: C-peptide and insulin are stored together in secretory granules within the beta cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why insulin is co-secreted with C-peptide.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OVERALL REGULATION OF GLUCOSE HOMEOSTASIS | INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS

items.assertion: Type 1 DM is characterized by immune-mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
items.reason: This process leads to insulin deficiency and the clinical manifestations of diabetes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN ACTION | TYPE1 DM

items.assertion: The presence of two or more autoantibodies against islet cell antigens indicates stage 3 type 1 DM.
items.reason: Stage 1 type 1 DM is designated by the presence of these autoantibodies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; two or more autoantibodies indicate stage 1, not stage 3.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · INSULIN ACTION | TYPE1 DM

items.assertion: Alpha cells are spared from autoimmune destruction in type 1 diabetes mellitus.
items.reason: Alpha cells, glucagon-producing cells, are functionally and embryologically similar to beta cells.
items.explanation: Despite their similarities, alpha cells are not destroyed by the autoimmune process.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: The presence of multiple ICAs in children indicates an immediate need for insulin therapy.
items.reason: Multiple ICAs indicate a high risk of developing type 1 DM within several years.
items.explanation: While multiple ICAs indicate a future risk, they do not necessitate immediate insulin therapy.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is characterized by impaired insulin secretion and insulin resistance.
items.reason: The pathophysiology of type 2 DM includes excessive hepatic glucose production, abnormal fat metabolism, and systemic low-grade inflammation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain the assertion.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: The genetic susceptibility to type 2 DM can be reliably predicted using known genetic loci.
items.reason: Genome-wide association studies have identified a large number of genetic loci associated with type 2 DM.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because it's not currently possible to predict type 2 DM using these loci, despite the reason being true.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Chronic hyperglycemia impairs islet function.
items.reason: This impairment leads to worsening of hyperglycemia, known as glucose toxicity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Increased adipocyte mass leads to decreased levels of circulating free fatty acids.
items.reason: Adipocytes secrete various biologic products including free fatty acids.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; increased adipocyte mass actually leads to increased levels of circulating free fatty acids.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Individuals with HNF-1β mutation require insulin treatment.
items.reason: These individuals have progressive impairment of insulin secretion and hepatic insulin resistance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these individuals require insulin treatment.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 1)

items.assertion: MODY caused by glucokinase gene mutations typically requires aggressive treatment.
items.reason: Glucokinase catalyzes the formation of glucose-6-phosphate from glucose, altering insulin secretion set point.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because individuals with glucokinase gene mutations have stable hyperglycemia that does not typically require aggressive treatment. The reason is true.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus
items.assertion: Pancreatic cancer is associated with new-onset type 2 diabetes.
items.reason: An episode of pancreatitis should prompt screening for diabetes.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the association between pancreatic cancer and new-onset type 2 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · OTHER FORMS OF DM (part 2) | Diabetes Mellitus

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
items.assertion: Type 1 DM can be associated with obesity.
items.reason: Some individuals with new-onset type 1 DM are overweight or obese.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT

items.subtopic: CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
items.assertion: Individuals with type 2 DM always require insulin therapy initially.
items.reason: Type 2 DM is characterized by insulin resistance rather than absolute insulin deficiency.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; not all individuals with type 2 DM require insulin therapy initially.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Diagnosis Classification and Pathophysiology · CLASSIFICATION OF DM IN AN INDIVIDUAL PATIENT
